text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY OFFICE VISIT NOTE
Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review and Follow-Up

---

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology / Melanoma Program
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, oncology follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Marcus Ellison is a 57-year-old White male with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV metastatic melanoma (T4b N3 M1c, AJCC 8th edition), primary site upper back, here today for routine oncology follow-up on nivolumab 480 mg IV q4 weeks maintenance, approximately 14 months since initiation of second-line immunotherapy. He was also discussed at today's multidisciplinary melanoma tumor board. He comes in accompanied by his wife and reports feeling well overall.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Ellison carries a diagnosis of nodular melanoma of the upper back, diagnosed at age 55, with initial staging revealing extensive locoregional and distant metastatic disease: bilateral axillary and left cervical lymphadenopathy, six discrete hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm in segment VII), and multiple cutaneous and subcutaneous satellite nodules across the trunk and extremities. LDH at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 642 U/L. His tumor harbored a BRAF p.V600E mutation confirmed both by immunohistochemistry (VE1) and comprehensive 523-gene NGS panel, with co-occurring alterations including CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PTEN p.R130* loss-of-function mutation, TP53 p.R175H gain-of-function mutation, TERT promoter mutation, RAC1 p.P29S, CCND1 amplification, and MYC amplification. TMB was intermediate-high at 18.4 mut/Mb; MSS. PD-L1 TPS 25%, CPS 38 by 28-8 assay.

Given the high-volume hepatic metastatic burden and dramatically elevated LDH at presentation — both factors favoring rapid disease control — he was initiated on first-line dabrafenib 150 mg BID / trametinib 2 mg QD (dabrafen/tram combination) with an excellent early response: LDH plummeted from 642 to 167 U/L by three months, and cross-sectional imaging confirmed robust partial response with target lesion reductions of 60-67%. His clinical course on dabrafenib/trametinib was complicated by a class-effect cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cuSCC) of the right forearm at approximately 4 months, managed with wide local excision with clear margins; he also experienced grade 1-2 fatigue and intermittent low-grade pyrexia throughout. Disease remained in stable partial response through months 6-7, but follow-up imaging at approximately 8 months demonstrated unequivocal radiographic progression, with the right axillary mass growing from 1.7 to 3.4 cm, hepatic segment VII lesion from 1.0 to 2.8 cm, appearance of two new hepatic lesions in segments IV and VI, multiple new subcutaneous nodules, and a new right external iliac lymph node — accompanied by resurgent LDH to 589 U/L. A resistance biopsy of a new abdominal subcutaneous nodule was performed, and repeat NGS confirmed acquired BRAF amplification (~8x copy number gain), a MAP2K1 p.P124S MEK1 resistance mutation (reducing drug binding), and a newly emergent PIK3CA p.E545K mutation, mechanistically explaining the resistance phenotype.

He was transitioned to second-line ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV q3 weeks plus nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV q3 weeks (CheckMate 067 dosing) after pre-immunotherapy brain MRI confirmed no CNS involvement. His immunotherapy course was complicated significantly at approximately 9 weeks (following cycle 3 of ipi/nivo) by grade 3 immune-mediated colitis, presenting with 8-10 watery bowel movements per day, severe cramping, dehydration, and fever. He was hospitalized and treated with high-dose IV methylprednisolone, and subsequently required infliximab 5 mg/kg x1 dose given incomplete steroid response. Both ipilimumab and nivolumab were held during this period; ipilimumab was permanently discontinued per protocol given the grade 3 severity. He responded well to infliximab — stool frequency normalized — and prednisone was successfully tapered over 8 weeks. Confirmatory colonoscopy at 10 weeks post-colitis onset demonstrated mucosal healing.

After approximately 4 months from immunotherapy initiation (following complete steroid taper), nivolumab was resumed as single-agent maintenance at the flat dose of 480 mg IV q4 weeks. He has now been on nivolumab maintenance for approximately 10 additional months. His first restaging CT at 5 months from immunotherapy start showed partial response with 53-57% reductions in target lesions. Deepening response was documented on subsequent imaging at 9 months (overall 77% reduction in target lesion sum from pre-immunotherapy baseline). A PET-CT at approximately 12 months confirmed near-complete metabolic response: no FDG-avid hepatic lesions, only a 0.8 cm residual right axillary soft tissue density with background-level SUVmax 2.1, felt to represent fibrotic/necrotic residual without viable disease.

His immunotherapy-related toxicities beyond the grade 3 colitis have included immune-mediated hypothyroidism, diagnosed at approximately 8 months into immunotherapy when TSH rose to 14.2 mIU/L with suppressed free T4. Anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies were both positive. He was uptitrated to levothyroxine 75 mcg/day with normalization of thyroid function (TSH 2.8-3.2 mIU/L on replacement) and resolution of symptoms (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, mild cognitive slowing). No corticosteroids were required.

He returns today having received his most recent nivolumab infusion 3 weeks ago. He reports feeling well, with markedly improved energy and quality of life compared to diagnosis. He has resumed modified light-duty landscaping work. He denies any gastrointestinal symptoms.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, night sweats, or significant unintentional weight change. Reports stable weight over the past 2 months. Energy level described as "8 out of 10" — good.
Gastrointestinal: Denies diarrhea, hematochezia, abdominal cramping, nausea, or vomiting. Bowel movements 1-2 formed stools per day, baseline for him. No symptoms suggestive of colitis recurrence.
Endocrine: Denies symptoms of hypothyroidism recurrence (no fatigue out of proportion, no cold intolerance, no constipation, no weight gain). Taking levothyroxine 75 mcg/day consistently each morning.
Skin/integument: Denies new skin lesions, rashes, or changes in existing moles. Stable scar at right forearm cuSCC excision site. No new hyperkeratotic lesions on palms (resolved after stopping dabrafenib).
Musculoskeletal: Denies new joint pain, swelling, or stiffness. No myalgias.
Neurological: Denies headache, visual disturbance, diplopia, focal weakness, ataxia, or cognitive changes. No symptoms suggestive of immune-related neurotoxicity.
Pulmonary: Denies cough, dyspnea, or chest pain. No symptoms suggestive of pneumonitis.
Hematologic: Denies easy bruising or bleeding. Reports improved exercise tolerance.
Urological: No dysuria, hematuria, or urinary frequency changes.
Ophthalmologic: No visual changes, eye pain, or photophobia.
Other: Denies oral mucositis, sicca symptoms.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-appearing, healthy-looking male in no acute distress. Mood upbeat. Accompanied by wife who is engaged and supportive.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 122/76 mmHg, heart rate 68 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 14, temperature 36.8°C, SpO2 99% on room air. Weight 82 kg (stable, up approximately 2 kg from nadir during colitis illness).
Performance Status: ECOG 0.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear, moist mucous membranes. No oral lesions. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae pink.
Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. Thyroid non-enlarged, non-tender, no nodules palpated.
Lymphatic: No palpable axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally. Previously palpable right axillary mass is no longer distinctly palpable; very subtle residual fibrous texture appreciated in the right axilla consistent with residual fibrotic change, no discrete mass. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.
Skin: Upper back primary excision site well-healed with stable scar. Right forearm WLE scar from cuSCC excision: well-healed, no signs of recurrence. Trunk and bilateral upper and lower extremities: no palpable subcutaneous nodules. No new pigmented lesions of concern on visual inspection. No rash. No palmar hyperkeratosis.
Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly on palpation. No abdominal masses. Normal bowel sounds.
Extremities: No edema. No cyanosis. No clubbing.
Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact. No ataxia. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 6.2 x10^9/L (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 13.9 g/dL (improved from prior nadir of 11.4 g/dL; now within normal range)
  - Platelets: 228 x10^9/L (normal)
  - Differential: normal without eosinophilia

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 139 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L, Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL, BUN: 14 mg/dL
  - AST: 24 U/L (normal), ALT: 28 U/L (normal), Alkaline phosphatase: 74 U/L (normal)
  - Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL (normal)
  - Albumin: 4.2 g/dL (normal)

LDH: 144 U/L (within normal limits; down from 642 U/L at initial presentation and 589 U/L at progression on BRAF/MEK inhibitor — an 87% reduction from peak)

Thyroid Function:
  - TSH: 2.6 mIU/L (within normal limits on levothyroxine 75 mcg/day)
  - Free T4: 1.2 ng/dL (normal)
  - (Free T3 not checked at this visit per protocol; last checked normal)

Corticosteroid taper: Prednisone has been fully tapered and discontinued (last dose approximately 2 months prior; patient confirmed off steroids without colitis recurrence).

Note: AM cortisol not re-checked at this visit (endocrinology confirmed adrenal axis intact during taper period).

---

IMAGING AND RECENT STUDIES

PET-CT (18F-FDG), performed approximately 2 months prior (at 12-month immunotherapy mark): Near-complete metabolic response documented. No FDG-avid hepatic lesions. Residual right axillary soft tissue measuring 0.8 cm with SUVmax 2.1, considered below threshold for active metabolic disease — interpreted as fibrotic/necrotic residual. No new hypermetabolic foci in any nodal basin, soft tissue, bone, or visceral organs. The dramatic reduction in overall metabolic disease burden compared to the baseline PET-CT at diagnosis (which showed at least 9 discrete hypermetabolic subcutaneous foci, hepatic lesions with SUVmax up to 14.3, and axillary nodal disease with SUVmax 18.7) is clinically extraordinary.

Brain MRI (prior study approximately 5 months ago): No intracranial metastases, no leptomeningeal enhancement. Surveillance MRI at the 6-month interval is due shortly.

ECG (at last visit): Normal sinus rhythm, no QTc prolongation (relevant to prior dabrafenib era; no longer on QTc-prolonging agents).

---

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD REVIEW SUMMARY

Mr. Ellison was presented at today's Melanoma Multidisciplinary Tumor Board attended by Medical Oncology, Surgical Oncology, Radiation Oncology, Diagnostic Radiology, Dermatology, and Pathology. The case was reviewed in full, including the near-complete metabolic PET-CT response, the patient's functional status, toxicity history, and current nivolumab maintenance.

Key discussion points:

1. Treatment holiday vs. continuation: There was robust discussion regarding whether to consider a planned immunotherapy holiday given the near-complete metabolic response. Arguments in favor of continuing: (a) initial M1c disease with very high-volume hepatic and nodal burden and elevated LDH — these features historically associate with higher relapse risk even in responders; (b) the patient initially received only partial combination (ipilimumab had to be permanently discontinued after 3 cycles due to grade 3 colitis, leaving nivolumab as the sole active agent for the majority of treatment); (c) published long-term outcomes from CheckMate 067 suggest durable remission rates of approximately 20% at 5 years, but durability of responses appears to favor continued treatment in M1c disease particularly when complete response has not been formally declared; (d) nivolumab is reasonably well-tolerated at present with no active grade ≥2 toxicities. Arguments for holiday: (a) near-complete metabolic response; (b) ongoing hypothyroidism as irreversible immune-related endocrinopathy; (c) patient quality of life and preference. Consensus recommendation: Continue nivolumab 480 mg IV q4 weeks indefinitely, with shared decision-making. The residual 0.8 cm axillary soft tissue with low-level metabolic activity, while likely inactive, has not met criteria for complete response, and the board was not comfortable recommending holiday in the setting of M1c disease without formal CR.

2. Role of re-biopsy: Re-biopsy of residual axillary soft tissue was discussed but deferred. The board agreed the PET-CT profile is consistent with treatment-related fibrosis, and biopsy risk (including potential bleeding and wound complications in a previously irradiated nodal field) was felt to outweigh benefit at this time.

3. Surveillance imaging interval: Imaging every 4 months on nivolumab maintenance endorsed. Brain MRI every 6 months endorsed. PET-CT to be repeated at 18-month immunotherapy mark (approximately 4 months from today) to further characterize residual axillary soft tissue.

4. Dermatology: Annual full-body dermatologic examination recommended, with particular attention to prior cuSCC site and any new BRAF inhibitor-independent squamous lesions.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Marcus Ellison is a 57-year-old male with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV metastatic melanoma (originally M1c with high hepatic and nodal burden, LDH 642 U/L) who has achieved a near-complete metabolic response on second-line nivolumab maintenance therapy (ongoing from the combination ipi/nivo induction from which ipilimumab was discontinued early due to grade 3 colitis). He is currently ECOG 0 with excellent quality of life and tolerating maintenance nivolumab without active immune-related adverse events. LDH is 144 U/L (normal). He is off steroids without colitis recurrence. Hypothyroidism is stable and well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.

This is a clinically gratifying response trajectory in a patient who presented with high-volume metastatic disease. Despite early ipilimumab discontinuation after 3 cycles, the ongoing durable benefit from nivolumab single-agent maintenance underscores that the immunotherapy has established durable immune control. Long-term outcomes data for M1c melanoma treated with combination IO followed by nivolumab maintenance demonstrate approximately 20% of patients achieving durable remission at 5 years; this patient is tracking toward the favorable end of the outcome spectrum.

Active issues and plans:

1. METASTATIC MELANOMA — near-complete metabolic response on nivolumab maintenance:
   - Continue nivolumab 480 mg IV flat dose q4 weeks per tumor board recommendation. Patient agreeable after thorough discussion of risks/benefits of continued therapy vs. holiday; he prefers to continue given his understanding of the high initial disease burden and desire to maintain disease control.
   - Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 4 months.
   - PET-CT (18F-FDG) repeat at the 18-month immunotherapy mark (approximately 4 months) to further evaluate residual right axillary soft tissue.
   - Pre-medication for nivolumab: acetaminophen 650 mg PO and diphenhydramine 25 mg PO per institutional protocol (though patient has not had infusion reactions).

2. IMMUNE-MEDIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM (permanent, on stable replacement):
   - Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg PO every morning, taken on empty stomach 30 minutes before meals.
   - TSH and free T4 to be rechecked in 3 months per thyroid monitoring protocol.
   - Endocrinology: last seen 3 months ago, annual follow-up scheduled.
   - Patient counseled to contact us if symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism develop (fatigue, weight change, palpitations, heat/cold intolerance).

3. IMMUNE-MEDIATED COLITIS HISTORY (resolved, grade 3, prior):
   - No active symptoms. Off steroids. Prednisone fully tapered and discontinued approximately 2 months prior.
   - Colonoscopy at 10-week post-colitis mark showed mucosal healing. No repeat colonoscopy planned unless symptoms recur.
   - Patient counseled again today on early warning signs of colitis recurrence: increased stool frequency above baseline, loose/watery stools, abdominal cramping, blood in stool. Instructed to contact triage line immediately if any of these occur and to avoid NSAIDs and avoid initiation of new medications without oncology/GI input.
   - Gastroenterology co-management: annual follow-up maintained.

4. CUTANEOUS SCC HISTORY (right forearm, resolved, grade 2 adverse event on BRAF inhibitor):
   - Patient is off dabrafenib; cuSCC risk substantially reduced off BRAF inhibitor therapy.
   - Dermatology surveillance: annual full-body skin exam. Next appointment in 8 months.
   - Patient instructed in monthly self-skin exam and to present for evaluation of any new or changing skin lesions.

5. BRAIN MRI SURVEILLANCE:
   - Last brain MRI approximately 5 months ago: no CNS disease.
   - Repeat brain MRI with and without contrast due in approximately 1 month (6-month surveillance interval). Order placed today.

6. GENERAL HEALTH MAINTENANCE AND QUALITY OF LIFE:
   - Performance status ECOG 0. Patient has returned to modified light-duty landscaping work, which is gratifying.
   - FACT-M quality of life assessment completed in clinic today — scores in excellent range, markedly improved from baseline.
   - Discussed sun-protective measures given melanoma history and prior extensive sun/UV occupational exposure: broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen daily, protective clothing, avoidance of peak UV hours. Patient acknowledges this is important given his occupational setting.
   - Discussed importance of hydration, balanced diet, and continued regular physical activity consistent with his current good performance status.
   - Smoking: former smoker, quit 12+ years ago. Counseled to remain smoke-free.

7. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Return to clinic in 4 weeks for next nivolumab infusion and clinical assessment.
   - Labs at next visit: CBC, CMP, LFTs, LDH, TSH.
   - Imaging as outlined above.
   - Patient and wife verbalize understanding of the plan and agree. Emergency contact information confirmed. Patient instructed to call the oncology triage line with any new symptoms, particularly GI, respiratory, or neurological changes.

---

Time spent: Approximately 50 minutes total, including 20 minutes for direct patient care, review of tumor board materials, and preparation of this note.

All questions answered. Patient and spouse express gratitude for care and remain engaged and hopeful.

[Dictated and signed by Attending Oncologist, Medical Oncology / Melanoma Program]
